header.topic: Building a hierarchical mind map to plan an extended piece of writing

learning_sequence_context.previous_lesson_summary: Last lesson pupils explored an existing mind-mapping tool's interface and annotated a pre-built example, identifying central node, branches and sub-branches.
learning_sequence_context.next_lesson_link: Next lesson pupils export their mind map and use it as the planning basis for a PowerPoint slide structure, beginning the PowerPoint unit.

expected_outcomes.lesson_objective: Pupils can construct a mind map with a central topic, at least three main branches, and at least two levels of sub-branching to represent a hierarchical structure of ideas.
assessment_criteria: Each student will create a central node clearly labelled with the main topic.
Each student will add at least three main branches representing distinct sub-themes.
Each student will add at least one further level of sub-branches beneath at least two main branches.
expected_outcomes.activity_summary: Individual construction of a mind map for a given writing topic, using the class mind-mapping software, followed by a paired peer-review swap.

key_vocabulary.definition: An ordering of ideas from general (central) to specific (sub-branches).
A line connecting related ideas in a mind map; distinct from its computing/algorithms meaning used later in KS4.

preparation.additional_adult_role: Support pupils who default to a linear list rather than a branching structure, using the printed worked example to prompt re-grouping.
accessibility: Printed worked example as an alternative to the on-screen model for pupils who benefit from a static reference

teaching_episode.teacher_actions: Display three unconnected topic words; ask pupils to jot down which they think should be 'central' and why.
teaching_episode.pupil_actions: Individually note their choice and one reason on mini-whiteboards.

check_for_understanding.question: Which of these three words is broad enough to sit at the centre of a mind map?
check_for_understanding.decision_rule: Proceed if most pupils identify the broadest term; otherwise briefly re-discuss general-to-specific ordering.

teaching_episode.teacher_actions: Model live construction of a central node and three main branches in the mind-mapping software for a sample topic.
teaching_episode.pupil_actions: Watch and identify verbally when a new branch versus a new sub-branch should be added.

check_for_understanding.question: Should this new idea be a main branch or a sub-branch of an existing one?
check_for_understanding.decision_rule: Move to guided practice regardless; use responses to judge how much modelling of sub-branching is needed next.

teaching_episode.teacher_actions: Add a second level of sub-branches to the live example, thinking aloud about how a specific detail relates back to its parent branch.
teaching_episode.pupil_actions: Observe; note one example of a sub-branch relationship they found convincing.

teaching_episode.teacher_actions: Set independent construction task using the writing topic prompt; circulate offering least-help-first prompts to pupils who have stalled at three branches with no sub-branching.
teaching_episode.pupil_actions: Independently construct a mind map for the given writing topic, aiming for the full assessment criteria.

check_for_understanding.question: Does your mind map have at least two branches with a further level of sub-branches beneath them?
check_for_understanding.decision_rule: Pupils below threshold at the 15-minute mark receive a targeted prompt referencing the worked example.

teaching_episode.teacher_actions: Facilitate paired peer review against the assessment criteria checklist; cold-call two pairs to share one strength and one improvement.
teaching_episode.pupil_actions: Swap with a partner, check the criteria checklist against the partner's mind map, and give one specific piece of feedback.

check_for_understanding.question: Exit ticket: how many levels of sub-branching does your final mind map have?
check_for_understanding.decision_rule: Pupils reporting only one level are flagged for a brief starter recap at the beginning of the next lesson before moving to PowerPoint.
